user_input: What are the seven layers of the OSI model in order from bottom to top?
retrieved_contexts: ['NATIONAL INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY JAIPUR Department of Computer Science and Engineering MODEL ANSWER SHEET Subject: Computer Networks (CS-401) | End Semester Examination 2024-25 Maximum Marks: 60 | Prepared by: Examination Cell Answer 1: OSI Reference Model [12 Marks] (a) Seven Layers of the OSI Model (bottom to top): Layer No. Layer Name PDU Name 1 Physical Layer Bits 2 Data Link Layer Frames 3 Network Layer Packets 4 Transport Layer Segments 5 Session Layer Data 6 Presentation Layer Data 7 Application Layer Data (b) Functions of Each Layer: Physical Layer (Layer 1): Responsible for the transmission and reception of raw bit streams over a physical medium. Deals with voltage levels, timing of voltage changes, physical data rates, and physical connectors. Examples: Ethernet cables, fiber optic cables, hubs, repeaters. Data Link Layer (Layer 2): Provides node-to-node data transfer between two directly connected nodes. Handles framing, physical addressing (MAC addresses), error detection (using CRC), and flow control. Examples: Ethernet, Wi-Fi (802.11), PPP, switches. Network Layer (Layer 3): Responsible for logical addressing, routing, and forwarding of packets from source to destination across multiple networks. Handles IP addressing, routing protocols, and packet fragmentation. Examples: IP, ICMP, routers. Transport Layer (Layer 4): Provides end-to-end communication services for applications. Handles segmentation, reassembly, flow control, error recovery, and multiplexing. Examples: TCP (reliable), UDP (unreliable but fast). Session Layer (Layer 5): Manages and controls the connections (sessions) between computers. Responsible for session establishment, maintenance, and termination. Also handles authentication and reconnection. Examples: NetBIOS, RPC. Presentation Layer (Layer 6): Responsible for data translation, encryption, and compression. Ensures data sent by the application layer of one system can be read by the application layer of another. Examples: SSL/TLS encryption, JPEG, MPEG, ASCII, EBCDIC. Application Layer (Layer 7): Provides network services directly to end-user applications. Acts as the interface between the network and the software application. Examples: HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, Telnet. (c) Encapsulation Process: As data travels down the OSI stack from the sender, each layer adds its own header (and sometimes trailer) to the data — this process is called encapsulation. At Layer 7 (Application), raw data is created. At Layer 4 (Transport), a TCP/UDP header with port numbers is added, forming a Segment. At Layer 3 (Network), an IP header with source and destination IP addresses is added, forming a Packet. At Layer 2 (Data Link), a MAC header and CRC trailer are added, forming a Frame. At Layer 1 (Physical), the frame is converted to bits and transmitted. At the receiver, the reverse process (decapsulation) occurs — each layer strips its own header as data moves up. (d) Protocol Examples: Network Layer (Layer 3): IP (Internet Protocol) — assigns logical addresses and routes packets across networks. Transport Layer (Layer 4): TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) — provides reliable, connection-oriented data transfer with error recovery and flow control. Answer 2: TCP vs UDP [10 Marks] (a) Connection-Oriented vs. Connectionless: Connection-oriented communication (used by TCP) establishes a dedicated logical connection between sender and receiver before any data is exchanged. A three-way handshake must complete successfully before data transfer begins. The connection is', "and broadcast addresses). Subnet 1: Network 192.168.10.0, Hosts 192.168.10.1 to 192.168.10.62, Broadcast 192.168.10.63. Subnet 2: Network 192.168.10.64, Hosts 192.168.10.65 to 192.168.10.126, Broadcast 192.168.10.127. Subnet 3: Network 192.168.10.128, Hosts 192.168.10.129 to 192.168.10.190, Broadcast 192.168.10.191. Subnet 4: Network 192.168.10.192, Hosts 192.168.10.193 to 192.168.10.254, Broadcast 192.168.10.255. (c) Classful vs Classless (CIDR) Addressing: Classful addressing divides IP addresses into fixed classes: Class A (1-126, /8, large networks), Class B (128-191, /16, medium networks), Class C (192-223, /24, small networks). This led to massive address wastage — a company needing 300 hosts had to use a Class B block (65,534 hosts). CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing) removes fixed class boundaries and allows any prefix length (e.g., /22, /27). This allows precise allocation of addresses matching actual need, dramatically reducing waste and extending IPv4 lifetime. Answer 4: Routing Algorithms [14 Marks] (a) Routing — Static vs Dynamic: Routing is the process of selecting a path for traffic in a network or across multiple networks. Routers use routing tables to forward packets toward their destinations. Static Routing: Routes are manually configured by a network administrator. They do not change unless manually updated. Advantages: simple, low overhead, secure. Disadvantages: not scalable, no automatic failure recovery. Used in small or stable networks. Dynamic Routing: Routers exchange routing information automatically using routing protocols and update their tables based on current network conditions. Advantages: adapts to failures, scalable. Disadvantages: more complex, consumes bandwidth. Examples: RIP, OSPF, BGP. (b) Dijkstra's Link State Routing Algorithm: Dijkstra's algorithm finds the shortest path from a source node to all other nodes. Each router has complete knowledge of the network topology (received via LSA flooding). Algorithm steps: Initialize — set distance to source as 0, all others as infinity. Mark source as visited. Relaxation — for the current node, update distances to all unvisited neighbors if the path through the current node is shorter. Mark visited — move to the unvisited node with the smallest tentative distance. Repeat until all nodes are visited. Example network: A-B(4), A-C(2), B-C(1), B-D(5), C-D(8), C-E(10), D-E(2). Starting from A: initial distances — B=inf, C=inf, D=inf, E=inf. Visit A: B=4, C=2. Visit C (dist=2): B=min(4,3)=3, D=10, E=12. Visit B (dist=3): D=min(10,8)=8. Visit D (dist=8): E=min(12,10)=10. Visit E (dist=10). Shortest paths from A: to B=3 (A-C-B), to C=2 (A-C), to D=8 (A-C-B-D), to E=10 (A-C-B-D-E). (c) Distance Vector Routing and Count-to-Infinity: In Distance Vector Routing (Bellman-Ford), each router maintains a table of distances to all destinations and shares this table with its direct neighbors periodically. Routers update their tables based on neighbors' tables using the formula: D(x,y) = min over all neighbors v of [cost(x,v) + D(v,y)]. Count-to-Infinity Problem: When a link fails, routers may incorrectly learn about the failure. For example, if A-B link fails, B may think it can reach A through C with cost 3 (C's old table says cost 2 to A). C then thinks it can reach A through B with cost 4. This loop continues, incrementing indefinitely until infinity (16 in RIP). Split Horizon Solution: A router does not advertise a route back to the neighbor from whom it", "transfer with error recovery and flow control. Answer 2: TCP vs UDP [10 Marks] (a) Connection-Oriented vs. Connectionless: Connection-oriented communication (used by TCP) establishes a dedicated logical connection between sender and receiver before any data is exchanged. A three-way handshake must complete successfully before data transfer begins. The connection is explicitly terminated after transfer. Connectionless communication (used by UDP) sends data packets (datagrams) without first establishing a connection. Each packet is independent and may take a different route. There is no guarantee of delivery, ordering, or duplicate protection. (b) TCP Three-Way Handshake: Step 1 — SYN: The client sends a TCP segment with the SYN (synchronize) flag set to the server, along with an initial sequence number (ISN). This initiates the connection request. Step 2 — SYN-ACK: The server responds with a segment that has both SYN and ACK flags set. The ACK number is client's ISN + 1. The server also sends its own ISN. Step 3 — ACK: The client sends a segment with the ACK flag set and acknowledgment number equal to server's ISN + 1. After this, the connection is established and data transfer can begin. (c) TCP vs UDP Comparison Table: Feature TCP UDP Connection Connection-oriented (3-way handshake) Connectionless Reliability Reliable — guarantees delivery Unreliable — no guarantee Ordering Ensures packets arrive in order No ordering guarantee Speed Slower due to overhead Faster, low overhead Error Checking Yes — retransmits lost packets Yes — but no retransmission Flow Control Yes (sliding window) No Header Size 20-60 bytes 8 bytes (d) Application Protocol Examples: TCP Protocols: (1) HTTP/HTTPS — web browsing requires reliable delivery of complete web pages. (2) FTP — file transfers must be complete and correct, making reliability essential. UDP Protocols: (1) DNS — queries are short and a retry is acceptable; speed is critical. (2) Online video streaming (RTP) — a few lost frames are acceptable; low latency is more important than perfect reliability. Answer 3: IP Addressing and Subnetting [12 Marks] (a) IP Address — IPv4 vs IPv6: An IP (Internet Protocol) address is a unique numerical label assigned to every device connected to a network that uses the Internet Protocol for communication. IPv4: 32-bit address written in dotted-decimal notation. Provides approximately 4.3 billion addresses. Example: 192.168.1.100. Divided into four octets (e.g., 11000000.10101000.00000001.01100100). IPv6: 128-bit address written in hexadecimal notation separated by colons. Provides 3.4 x 10^38 addresses — practically unlimited. Example: 2001:0db8:85a3:0000:0000:8a2e:0370:7334. Introduced to solve IPv4 address exhaustion. Also includes built-in security (IPSec) and better multicast support. (b) Subnetting — 192.168.10.0/24 into 4 subnets: Original network: 192.168.10.0/24 (subnet mask 255.255.255.0, 256 addresses). To create 4 subnets we need 2 additional bits (2^2 = 4). New prefix: /26. New subnet mask: 255.255.255.192 (11111111.11111111.11111111.11000000). Each subnet has 64 addresses — 62 usable hosts (subtract network and broadcast addresses). Subnet 1: Network 192.168.10.0, Hosts 192.168.10.1 to 192.168.10.62, Broadcast 192.168.10.63. Subnet 2: Network 192.168.10.64, Hosts 192.168.10.65 to 192.168.10.126, Broadcast 192.168.10.127. Subnet 3: Network 192.168.10.128, Hosts 192.168.10.129 to 192.168.10.190, Broadcast 192.168.10.191. Subnet 4: Network 192.168.10.192, Hosts 192.168.10.193 to 192.168.10.254, Broadcast 192.168.10.255. (c) Classful vs Classless (CIDR) Addressing: Classful"]
response:
The seven layers of the OSI model, in order from bottom to top, are:

1. Physical Layer
2. Data Link Layer
3. Network Layer
4. Transport Layer
5. Session Layer
6. Presentation Layer
7. Application Layer
reference: The seven layers of the OSI model from bottom to top are: Physical Layer, Data Link Layer, Network Layer, Transport Layer, Session Layer, Presentation Layer, and Application Layer.

response:
The TCP three-way handshake process is a connection establishment procedure used by the Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) to establish a dedicated logical connection between a sender and a receiver before any data is exchanged. The process involves the following three steps:

1. **SYN (Synchronize)**: The client (sender) sends a TCP segment with the SYN flag set to the server (receiver), along with an initial sequence number (ISN). This initiates the connection request.
2. **SYN-ACK (Synchronize-Acknowledgment)**: The server responds with a segment that has both SYN and ACK flags set. The ACK number is the client's ISN + 1, indicating that the server has acknowledged the client's initial sequence number. The server also sends its own ISN.
3. **ACK (Acknowledgment)**: The client sends a segment with the ACK flag set, and the acknowledgment number is set to the server's ISN + 1. This confirms that the client has received the server's SYN-ACK segment and is ready to establish the connection.

After the three-way handshake is complete, the connection is established, and data transfer can begin. The three-way handshake ensures that both the client and server are aware of each other's sequence numbers and are ready to communicate, providing a reliable and connection-oriented communication channel.
reference: The TCP three-way handshake has three steps. Step 1 (SYN): the client sends a segment with the SYN flag set and an initial sequence number (ISN). Step 2 (SYN-ACK): the server responds with both SYN and ACK flags set, acknowledging the client's ISN + 1 and sending its own ISN. Step 3 (ACK): the client sends a segment with the ACK flag set and acknowledgment number equal to the server's ISN + 1. After this the connection is established and data transfer can begin.

Answer 4: Routing Algorithms [14 Marks] (a) Routing — Static vs Dynamic: Routing is the process of selecting a path for traffic in a network or across multiple networks. Routers use routing tables to forward packets toward their destinations. Static Routing: Routes are manually configured by a network administrator. They do not change unless manually updated. Advantages: simple, low overhead, secure. Disadvantages: not scalable, no automatic failure recovery. Used in small or stable networks. Dynamic Routing: Routers exchange routing information automatically using routing protocols and update their tables based on current network conditions. Advantages: adapts to failures, scalable. Disadvantages: more complex, consumes bandwidth. Examples: RIP, OSPF, BGP. (b) Dijkstra's Link State Routing Algorithm: Dijkstra's algorithm finds the shortest path from a source node to all other nodes. Each router has complete knowledge of the network topology (received via LSA flooding). Algorithm steps: Initialize — set distance to source as 0, all others as infinity. Mark source as visited. Relaxation — for the current node, update distances to all unvisited neighbors if the path through the current node is shorter. Mark visited — move to the unvisited node with the smallest tentative distance. Repeat until all nodes are visited. Example network: A-B(4), A-C(2), B-C(1), B-D(5), C-D(8), C-E(10), D-E(2). Deals with voltage levels, timing of voltage changes, physical data rates, and physical connectors. Examples: Ethernet cables, fiber optic cables, hubs, repeaters. Data Link Layer (Layer 2): Provides node-to-node data transfer between two directly connected nodes. Handles framing, physical addressing (MAC addresses), error detection (using CRC), and flow control. Examples: Ethernet, Wi-Fi (802.11), PPP, switches. Network Layer (Layer 3): Responsible for logical addressing, routing, and forwarding of packets from source to destination across multiple networks. Handles IP addressing, routing protocols, and packet fragmentation. Examples: IP, ICMP, routers. Transport Layer (Layer 4): Provides end-to-end communication services for applications. Handles segmentation, reassembly, flow control, error recovery, and multiplexing. Examples: TCP (reliable), UDP (unreliable but fast). Session Layer (Layer 5): Manages and controls the connections (sessions) between computers. Responsible for session establishment, maintenance, and termination. Also handles authentication and reconnection. Examples: NetBIOS, RPC. Presentation Layer (Layer 6): Responsible for data translation, encryption, and compression. Ensures data sent by the application layer of one system can be read by the application layer of another. Examples: SSL/TLS encryption, JPEG, MPEG, ASCII, EBCDIC. Application Layer (Layer 7): Provides network services directly to end-user applications. Acts as the interface between the network and the software application. Examples: HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, Telnet. (c) Encapsulation Process: As data travels down the OSI stack from the sender, each layer adds its own header (and sometimes trailer) to the data — this process is called encapsulation. At Layer 7 (Application), raw data is created. At Layer 4 (Transport), a TCP/UDP header with port numbers is added, forming a Segment. At Layer 3 (Network), an IP header with source and destination IP addresses is added, forming a Packet. At Layer 2 (Data Link), a MAC header and CRC trailer are added, forming a Frame. To divide the network 192.168.10.0/24 into 4 subnets, we need to determine the subnet mask and the IP address range for each subnet.

The network 192.168.10.0/24 has a total of 256 (2^8) possible IP addresses, ranging from 192.168.10.0 to 192.168.10.255.

To divide this network into 4 subnets, we can use a subnet mask of /26, which will give us 4 subnets with 64 (2^6) possible IP addresses each.

Here are the 4 subnets:

Subnet 1:
Network: 192.168.10.0
Hosts: 192.168.10.1 to 192.168.10.62
Broadcast: 192.168.10.63

Subnet 2:
Network: 192.168.10.64
Hosts: 192.168.10.65 to 192.168.10.126
Broadcast: 192.168.10.127

Subnet 3:
Network: 192.168.10.128
Hosts: 192.168.10.129 to 192.168.10.190
Broadcast: 192.168.10.191

Subnet 4:
Network: 192.168.10.192
Hosts: 192.168.10.193 to 192.168.10.254
Broadcast: 192.168.10.255

Note that the subnet mask /26 is equivalent to 255.255.255.192.
reference: To create 4 subnets you need 2 additional bits (2^2 = 4), giving a new prefix of /26 and a subnet mask of 255.255.255.192. Each subnet has 64 addresses with 62 usable hosts. Subnet 1 is 192.168.10.0 with hosts 192.168.10.1 to 192.168.10.62 and broadcast 192.168.10.63; subnet 2 starts at 192.168.10.64, and so on in blocks of 64.
